attributes.io.input:
{"description":"Explore project structure","prompt":"Explore the project at /home/user/workspace thoroughly. I need to understand:

1. Read package.json, tsconfig.json, next.config.js/ts/mjs
2. List ALL files in the `pages/` directory recursively with their full paths
3. Check if there's already an `app/` directory
4. Check if node_modules/next/dist/docs/ exists and list its contents (per project instructions)
5. Check for any middleware, layouts, or other special Next.js files
6. Read a few sample page files to understand the patterns used

Be very thorough - I need the complete file tree of pages/ and any existing app/ directory.","subagent_type":"general-purpose"}
attributes.io.output:
[{"type":"text","text":"Here is the complete exploration of the project at `/home/user/workspace`.

---

## 1. Configuration Files

**package.json** (`/home/user/workspace/package.json`)
- Type: `"module"` (ESM)
- Dependencies: `next` (16.2.0-canary.41), `react` (19.1.0), `react-dom` (19.1.0), `ai` (5.0.59), `@ai-sdk/react` (2.0.0-beta.28), `@modelcontextprotocol/sdk` (^1.17.0), `zod` (^4.0.10)
- DevDependencies: `typescript` (^5), `vitest` (^3.1.3), `@vitejs/plugin-react` (^4.4.1), `vite-tsconfig-paths` (^5.1.4), `@types/node`, `@types/react`, `@types/react-dom`
- Scripts: `dev` (next dev), `build` (next build), `start` (next start)

**tsconfig.json** (`/home/user/workspace/tsconfig.json`)
- Target: ES2017, JSX: `react-jsx`, Module: `esnext`, ModuleResolution: `bundler`
- Path alias: `@/*` maps to `./*`
- Includes: `next-env.d.ts`, `**/*.ts`, `**/*.tsx`, `.next/types/**/*.ts`

**next.config.ts** (`/home/user/workspace/next.config.ts`)
- Minimal -- just `import type { NextConfig } from "next"` with an empty config object `{}`

---

## 2. Complete `pages/` Directory Tree

```
pages/
  pages/404.js                         -- Custom 404 page
  pages/_app.js                        -- Custom App component (wraps with AppProvider, header/nav/footer)
  pages/_document.js                   -- Custom Document (HTML shell, fonts, meta)
  pages/_error.js                      -- Custom Error page
  pages/api/
    pages/api/posts/
      pages/api/posts/[id].js          -- API route: GET/PUT/DELETE for single post
      pages/api/posts/index.js         -- API route: GET (list)/POST (create) posts
  pages/blog/
    pages/blog/[id].js                 -- Dynamic blog post page (SSG with getStaticPaths + getStaticProps, fallback 'blocking', ISR 300s)
    pages/blog/index.js                -- Blog index page (SSG with getStaticProps, ISR 60s)
  pages/index.js                       -- Home page (SSR with getServerSideProps, fetches 5 posts from JSONPlaceholder)
```

All page files use `.js` extension (JavaScript, not TypeScript).

---

## 3. No `app/` Directory

There is **no `app/` directory** in the project. This is a **Pages Router** project only.

---

## 4. Next.js Docs Directory (`node_modules/next/dist/docs/`)

Exists and contains comprehensive documentation organized by route:

**`/home/user/workspace/node_modules/next/dist/docs/`**
```
index.mdx
01-app/           -- App Router documentation
  01-getting-started/  (18 guides: installation through upgrading)
  02-guides/           (various guides)
  03-api-reference/
  04-glossary.mdx
02-pages/         -- Pages Router documentation
  01-getting-started/
  02-guides/
  03-building-your-application/
  04-api-reference/
03-architecture/
04-community/
```

Per the project instructions in `AGENTS.md`, you should read the relevant guide from this directory before writing any code.

---

## 5. Special Next.js Files

- **No middleware** found anywhere in the project.
- **No layout, loading, error, not-found, template, or instrumentation files** found (these are App Router concepts).
- The project uses the Pages Router equivalents:
  - `/pages/_app.js` -- App wrapper (layout equivalent)
  - `/pages/_document.js` -- HTML document shell
  - `/pages/_error.js` -- Global error page
  - `/pages/404.js` -- Custom 404 page

---

## 6. Project Patterns

**Root-level files:**
```
/home/user/workspace/
  AGENTS.md              -- agent instructions (CLAUDE.md is a symlink to this)
  next.config.ts
  package.json
  tsconfig.json
  .gitignore

  /components/
    AppProvider.js       -- React context provider (theme state, uses createContext/useState)

  /styles/
    globals.css          -- Global CSS (Inter font, grid layout, card styling, error pages)
```

**Data fetching patterns used:**
- `getServerSideProps` -- Home page (`pages/index.js`)
- `getStaticProps` + `getStaticPaths` with `fallback: 'blo…(+843)

1 --- 2 title: page.js 3 description: API reference for the page.js file. 4 --- 5 6 The `page` file allows you to define UI that is **unique** to a route. You can create a page by default exporting a component from the file: 7 8 ```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" switcher 9 export default function Page({ 10 params, 11 searchParams, 12 }: { 13 params: Promise<{ slug: string }> 14 searchParams: Promise<{ [key: string]: string | string[] | undefined }> 15 }) { 16 return

My Page

17 } 18 ``` 19 20 ```jsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.js" switcher 21 export default function Page({ params, searchParams }) { 22 return

My Page

23 } 24 ``` 25 26 ## Good to know 27 28 - The `.js`, `.jsx`, or `.tsx` file extensions can be used for `page`. 29 - A `page` is always the **leaf** of the route subtree. 30 - A `page` file is required to make a route segment **publicly accessible**. 31 - Pages are [Server Components](https://react.dev/reference/rsc/server-components) by default, but can be set to a [Client Component](https://react.dev/reference/rsc/use-client). 32 33 ## Reference 34 35 ### Props 36 37 #### `params` (optional) 38 39 A promise that resolves to an object containing the [dynamic route parameters](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/dynamic-routes) from the root segment down to that page. 40 41 ```tsx filename="app/shop/[slug]/page.tsx" switcher 42 export default async function Page({ 43 params, 44 }: { 45 params: Promise<{ slug: string }> 46 }) { 47 const { slug } = await params 48 } 49 ``` 50 51 ```jsx filename="app/shop/[slug]/page.js" switcher 52 export default async function Page({ params }) { 53 const { slug } = await params 54 } 55 ``` 56 57 | Example Route | URL | `params` | 58 | ------------------------------------ | ----------- | --------------------------------------- | 59 | `app/shop/[slug]/page.js` | `/shop/1` | `Promise<{ slug: '1' }>` | 60 | `app/shop/[category]/[item]/page.js` | `/shop/1/2` | `Promise<{ category: '1', item: '2' }>` | 61 | `app/shop/[...slug]/page.js` | `/shop/1/2` | `Promise<{ slug: ['1', '2'] }>` | 62 63 - Since the `params` prop is a promise, you must use `async/await` or React's [`use`](https://react.dev/reference/react/use) function to access the values. 64 - In version 14 and earlier, `params` was a synchronous prop. To help with backwards compatibility, you can still access it synchronously in Next.js 15, but this behavior will be deprecated in the future. 65 66 #### `searchParams` (optional) 67 68 A promise that resolves to an object containing the [search parameters](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Learn/Common_questions/What_is_a_URL#parameters) of the current URL. For example: 69 70 ```tsx filename="app/shop/page.tsx" switcher 71 export default async function Page({ 72 searchParams, 73 }: { 74 searchParams: Promise<{ [key: string]: string | string[] | undefined }> 75 }) { 76 const filters = (await searchParams).filters 77 } 78 ``` 79 80 ```jsx filename="app/shop/page.js" switcher 81 export default async function Page({ searchParams }) { 82 const filters = (await searchParams).filters 83 } 84 ``` 85 86 Client Component **pages** can also access `searchParams` using React’s [`use`](https://react.dev/reference/react/use) hook: 87 88 ```tsx filename="app/shop/page.tsx" switcher 89 'use client' 90 import { use } from 'react' 91 92 export default function Page({ 93 searchParams, 94 }: { 95 searchParams: Promise<{ [key: string]: string | string[] | undefined }> 96 }) { 97 const filters = use(searchParams).filters 98 } 99 ``` 100 101 ```jsx filename="app/page.jsx" switcher 102 'use client' 103 import { use } from 'react' 104 105 export default function Page({ searchParams }) { 106 const filters = use(searchParams).filters 107 } 108 ``` 109 110 | Example URL | `searchParams` | 111 | -…(+5092)

attributes.gen_ai.operation.name: execute_tool
attributes.io.output:
1	---
2	title: layout.js
3	description: API reference for the layout.js file.
4	---
5	
6	The `layout` file is used to define a layout in your Next.js application.
7	
8	```tsx filename="app/dashboard/layout.tsx" switcher
9	export default function DashboardLayout({
10	  children,
11	}: {
12	  children: React.ReactNode
13	}) {
14	  return <section>{children}</section>
15	}
16	```
17	
18	```jsx filename="app/dashboard/layout.js" switcher
19	export default function DashboardLayout({ children }) {
20	  return <section>{children}</section>
21	}
22	```
23	
24	A **root layout** is the top-most layout in the root `app` directory. It is used to define the `<html>` and `<body>` tags and other globally shared UI.
25	
26	```tsx filename="app/layout.tsx" switcher
27	export default function RootLayout({
28	  children,
29	}: {
30	  children: React.ReactNode
31	}) {
32	  return (
33	    <html lang="en">
34	      <body>{children}</body>
35	    </html>
36	  )
37	}
38	```
39	
40	```jsx filename="app/layout.js" switcher
41	export default function RootLayout({ children }) {
42	  return (
43	    <html lang="en">
44	      <body>{children}</body>
45	    </html>
46	  )
47	}
48	```
49	
50	## Reference
51	
52	### Props
53	
54	#### `children` (required)
55	
56	Layout components should accept and use a `children` prop. During rendering, `children` will be populated with the route segments the layout is wrapping. These will primarily be the component of a child [Layout](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page) (if it exists) or [Page](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page), but could also be other special files like [Loading](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/loading) or [Error](/docs/app/getting-started/error-handling) when applicable.
57	
58	#### `params` (optional)
59	
60	A promise that resolves to an object containing the [dynamic route parameters](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/dynamic-routes) object from the root segment down to that layout.
61	
62	```tsx filename="app/dashboard/[team]/layout.tsx" switcher
63	export default async function Layout({
64	  children,
65	  params,
66	}: {
67	  children: React.ReactNode
68	  params: Promise<{ team: string }>
69	}) {
70	  const { team } = await params
71	}
72	```
73	
74	```jsx filename="app/dashboard/[team]/layout.js" switcher
75	export default async function Layout({ children, params }) {
76	  const { team } = await params
77	}
78	```
79	
80	| Example Route                     | URL            | `params`                           |
81	| --------------------------------- | -------------- | ---------------------------------- |
82	| `app/dashboard/[team]/layout.js`  | `/dashboard/1` | `Promise<{ team: '1' }>`           |
83	| `app/shop/[tag]/[item]/layout.js` | `/shop/1/2`    | `Promise<{ tag: '1', item: '2' }>` |
84	| `app/blog/[...slug]/layout.js`    | `/blog/1/2`    | `Promise<{ slug: ['1', '2'] }>`    |
85	
86	- Since the `params` prop is a promise. You must use `async/await` or React's [`use`](https://react.dev/reference/react/use) function to access the values.
87	  - In version 14 and earlier, `params` was a synchronous prop. To help with backwards compatibility, you can still access it synchronously in Next.js 15, but this behavior will be deprecated in the future.
88	
89	### Layout Props Helper
90	
91	You can type layouts with `LayoutProps` to get a strongly typed `params` and named slots inferred from your directory structure. `LayoutProps` is a globally available helper.
92	
93	```tsx filename="app/dashboard/layout.tsx"
94	export default function Layout(props: LayoutProps<'/dashboard'>) {
95	  return (
96	    <section>
97	      {props.children}
98	      {/* If you have app/dashboard/@analytics, it appears as a typed slot: */}
99	      {/* {props.analytics} */}
100	    </section>
101	  )
102	}
103	```
104	
105	> **Good to know**:
106	>
107	> - Types are generated during `next dev`, `next build` or `next typegen`.
108	> - After type generation, the `LayoutProps` helper is …(+17233)

attributes.io.output:
1	---
2	title: route.js
3	description: API reference for the route.js special file.
4	---
5	
6	Route Handlers allow you to create custom request handlers for a given route using the Web [Request](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/Request) and [Response](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/Response) APIs.
7	
8	```ts filename="route.ts" switcher
9	export async function GET() {
10	  return Response.json({ message: 'Hello World' })
11	}
12	```
13	
14	```js filename="route.js" switcher
15	export async function GET() {
16	  return Response.json({ message: 'Hello World' })
17	}
18	```
19	
20	## Reference
21	
22	### HTTP Methods
23	
24	A **route** file allows you to create custom request handlers for a given route. The following [HTTP methods](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/HTTP/Methods) are supported: `GET`, `POST`, `PUT`, `PATCH`, `DELETE`, `HEAD`, and `OPTIONS`.
25	
26	```ts filename="route.ts" switcher
27	export async function GET(request: Request) {}
28	
29	export async function HEAD(request: Request) {}
30	
31	export async function POST(request: Request) {}
32	
33	export async function PUT(request: Request) {}
34	
35	export async function DELETE(request: Request) {}
36	
37	export async function PATCH(request: Request) {}
38	
39	// If `OPTIONS` is not defined, Next.js will automatically implement `OPTIONS` and set the appropriate Response `Allow` header depending on the other methods defined in the Route Handler.
40	export async function OPTIONS(request: Request) {}
41	```
42	
43	```js filename="route.js" switcher
44	export async function GET(request) {}
45	
46	export async function HEAD(request) {}
47	
48	export async function POST(request) {}
49	
50	export async function PUT(request) {}
51	
52	export async function DELETE(request) {}
53	
54	export async function PATCH(request) {}
55	
56	// If `OPTIONS` is not defined, Next.js will automatically implement `OPTIONS` and set the appropriate Response `Allow` header depending on the other methods defined in the Route Handler.
57	export async function OPTIONS(request) {}
58	```
59	
60	### Parameters
61	
62	#### `request` (optional)
63	
64	The `request` object is a [NextRequest](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/next-request) object, which is an extension of the Web [Request](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/Request) API. `NextRequest` gives you further control over the incoming request, including easily accessing `cookies` and an extended, parsed, URL object `nextUrl`.
65	
66	```ts filename="route.ts" switcher
67	import type { NextRequest } from 'next/server'
68	
69	export async function GET(request: NextRequest) {
70	  const url = request.nextUrl
71	}
72	```
73	
74	```js filename="route.js" switcher
75	export async function GET(request) {
76	  const url = request.nextUrl
77	}
78	```
79	
80	#### `context` (optional)
81	
82	- **`params`**: a promise that resolves to an object containing the [dynamic route parameters](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/dynamic-routes) for the current route.
83	
84	```ts filename="app/dashboard/[team]/route.ts" switcher
85	export async function GET(
86	  request: Request,
87	  { params }: { params: Promise<{ team: string }> }
88	) {
89	  const { team } = await params
90	}
91	```
92	
93	```js filename="app/dashboard/[team]/route.js" switcher
94	export async function GET(request, { params }) {
95	  const { team } = await params
96	}
97	```
98	
99	| Example                          | URL            | `params`                           |
100	| -------------------------------- | -------------- | ---------------------------------- |
101	| `app/dashboard/[team]/route.js`  | `/dashboard/1` | `Promise<{ team: '1' }>`           |
102	| `app/shop/[tag]/[item]/route.js` | `/shop/1/2`    | `Promise<{ tag: '1', item: '2' }>` |
103	| `app/blog/[...slug]/route.js`    | `/blog/1/2`    | `Promise<{ slug: ['1', '2'] }>`    |
104	
105	### Route Context Helper
106	
107	You can type the Route Handler context using `RouteContext` to get…(+18603)

1 --- 2 title: error.js 3 description: API reference for the error.js special file. 4 related: 5 title: Learn more about error handling 6 links: 7 - app/getting-started/error-handling 8 --- 9 10 An **error** file allows you to handle unexpected runtime errors and display fallback UI. 11 12 19 20 ```tsx filename="app/dashboard/error.tsx" switcher 21 'use client' // Error boundaries must be Client Components 22 23 import { useEffect } from 'react' 24 25 export default function Error({ 26 error, 27 reset, 28 }: { 29 error: Error & { digest?: string } 30 reset: () => void 31 }) { 32 useEffect(() => { 33 // Log the error to an error reporting service 34 console.error(error) 35 }, [error]) 36 37 return ( 38
39

Something went wrong!

40 48
49 ) 50 } 51 ``` 52 53 ```jsx filename="app/dashboard/error.js" switcher 54 'use client' // Error boundaries must be Client Components 55 56 import { useEffect } from 'react' 57 58 export default function Error({ error, reset }) { 59 useEffect(() => { 60 // Log the error to an error reporting service 61 console.error(error) 62 }, [error]) 63 64 return ( 65
66

Something went wrong!

67 75
76 ) 77 } 78 ``` 79 80 `error.js` wraps a route segment and its nested children in a [React Error Boundary](https://react.dev/reference/react/Component#catching-rendering-errors-with-an-error-boundary). When an error throws within the boundary, the `error` component shows as the fallback UI. 81 82 89 90 > **Good to know**: 91 > 92 > - The [React DevTools](https://react.dev/learn/react-developer-tools) allow you to toggle error boundaries to test error states. 93 > - If you want errors to bubble up to the parent error boundary, you can `throw` when rendering the `error` component. 94 95 ## Reference 96 97 ### Props 98 99 #### `error` 100 101 An instance of an [`Error`](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/Error) object forwarded to the `error.js` Client Component. 102 103 > **Good to know:** During development, the `Error` object forwarded to the client will be serialized and include the `message` of the original error for easier debugging. However, **this behavior is different in production** to avoid leaking potentially sensitive details included in the error to the client. 104 105 #### `error.message` 106 107 - Errors forwarded from Client Components show the original `Error` message. 108 - Errors forwarded from Server Components show a generic message with an identifier. This is to prevent leaking sensitive details. You can use the identifier, under `errors.digest`, to match the corresponding server-side logs. 109 110 #### `error.digest` 111 112 An automatically generated hash of the error thrown. It can be used to match the corresponding error in server-side logs. 113 114 #### `reset` 115 116 The cause of an error can sometimes be temporary. In these cases, trying again might resolve the issue. 117 118 An error component can use the `reset()` function to prompt the user to attempt to recover from the error. When executed, the function will try to re-render the error boundary's contents. If successful, t…(+6856)

1 --- 2 title: not-found.js 3 description: API reference for the not-found.js file. 4 --- 5 6 Next.js provides two conventions to handle not found cases: 7 8 - **`not-found.js`**: Used when you call the [`notFound`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/not-found) function in a route segment. 9 - **`global-not-found.js`**: Used to define a global 404 page for unmatched routes across your entire app. This is handled at the routing level and doesn't depend on rendering a layout or page. 10 11 ## `not-found.js` 12 13 The **not-found** file is used to render UI when the [`notFound`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/not-found) function is thrown within a route segment. Along with serving a custom UI, Next.js will return a `200` HTTP status code for streamed responses, and `404` for non-streamed responses. 14 15 ```tsx filename="app/not-found.tsx" switcher 16 import Link from 'next/link' 17 18 export default function NotFound() { 19 return ( 20
21

Not Found

22

Could not find requested resource

23 Return Home 24
25 ) 26 } 27 ``` 28 29 ```jsx filename="app/blog/not-found.js" switcher 30 import Link from 'next/link' 31 32 export default function NotFound() { 33 return ( 34
35

Not Found

36

Could not find requested resource

37 Return Home 38
39 ) 40 } 41 ``` 42 43 ## `global-not-found.js` (experimental) 44 45 The `global-not-found.js` file lets you define a 404 page for your entire application. Unlike `not-found.js`, which works at the route level, this is used when a requested URL doesn't match any route at all. Next.js **skips rendering** and directly returns this global page. 46 47 The `global-not-found.js` file bypasses your app's normal rendering, which means you'll need to import any global styles, fonts, or other dependencies that your 404 page requires. 48 49 > **Good to know**: A smaller version of your global styles, and a simpler font family could improve performance of this page. 50 51 `global-not-found.js` is useful when you can't build a 404 page using a combination of `layout.js` and `not-found.js`. This can happen in two cases: 52 53 - Your app has multiple root layouts (e.g. `app/(admin)/layout.tsx` and `app/(shop)/layout.tsx`), so there's no single layout to compose a global 404 from. 54 - Your root layout is defined using top-level dynamic segments (e.g. `app/[country]/layout.tsx`), which makes composing a consistent 404 page harder. 55 56 To enable it, add the `globalNotFound` flag in `next.config.ts`: 57 58 ```tsx filename="next.config.ts" 59 import type { NextConfig } from 'next' 60 61 const nextConfig: NextConfig = { 62 experimental: { 63 globalNotFound: true, 64 }, 65 } 66 67 export default nextConfig 68 ``` 69 70 Then, create a file in the root of the `app` directory: `app/global-not-found.js`: 71 72 ```tsx filename="app/global-not-found.tsx" switcher 73 // Import global styles and fonts 74 import './globals.css' 75 import { Inter } from 'next/font/google' 76 import type { Metadata } from 'next' 77 78 const inter = Inter({ subsets: ['latin'] }) 79 80 export const metadata: Metadata = { 81 title: '404 - Page Not Found', 82 description: 'The page you are looking for does not exist.', 83 } 84 85 export default function GlobalNotFound() { 86 return ( 87 88 89

404 - Page Not Found

90

This page does not exist.

91 92 93 ) 94 } 95 ``` 96 97 ```jsx filename="app/global-not-found.js" switcher 98 // Import global styles and fonts 99 import './globals.css' 100 import { Inter } from 'next/font/google' 101 102 const inter = Inter({ subsets: ['latin'] }) 103 104 export const metadata = { 105 title: '404 - Page Not Found', 106 description: 'The page you are looking for does not exist.', 107 } 108 109 export d…(+4099)

1 --- 2 title: loading.js 3 description: API reference for the loading.js file. 4 --- 5 6 The special file `loading.js` helps you create meaningful Loading UI with [React Suspense](https://react.dev/reference/react/Suspense). With this convention, you can show an [instant loading state](#instant-loading-states) from the server while the content of a route segment streams in. The new content is automatically swapped in once complete. 7 8 15 16 ```tsx filename="app/feed/loading.tsx" switcher 17 export default function Loading() { 18 // Or a custom loading skeleton component 19 return

Loading...

20 } 21 ``` 22 23 ```jsx filename="app/feed/loading.js" switcher 24 export default function Loading() { 25 // Or a custom loading skeleton component 26 return

Loading...

27 } 28 ``` 29 30 Inside the `loading.js` file, you can add any light-weight loading UI. You may find it helpful to use the [React Developer Tools](https://react.dev/learn/react-developer-tools) to manually toggle Suspense boundaries. 31 32 By default, this file is a [Server Component](/docs/app/getting-started/server-and-client-components) - but can also be used as a Client Component through the `"use client"` directive. 33 34 ## Reference 35 36 ### Parameters 37 38 Loading UI components do not accept any parameters. 39 40 ## Behavior 41 42 ### Navigation 43 44 - The Fallback UI is [prefetched](/docs/app/getting-started/linking-and-navigating#prefetching), making navigation immediate unless prefetching hasn't completed. 45 - Navigation is interruptible, meaning changing routes does not need to wait for the content of the route to fully load before navigating to another route. 46 - Shared layouts remain interactive while new route segments load. 47 48 ### Instant Loading States 49 50 An instant loading state is fallback UI that is shown immediately upon navigation. You can pre-render loading indicators such as skeletons and spinners, or a small but meaningful part of future screens such as a cover photo, title, etc. This helps users understand the app is responding and provides a better user experience. 51 52 Create a loading state by adding a `loading.js` file inside a folder. 53 54 61 62 ```tsx filename="app/dashboard/loading.tsx" switcher 63 export default function Loading() { 64 // You can add any UI inside Loading, including a Skeleton. 65 return 66 } 67 ``` 68 69 ```jsx filename="app/dashboard/loading.js" switcher 70 export default function Loading() { 71 // You can add any UI inside Loading, including a Skeleton. 72 return 73 } 74 ``` 75 76 In the same folder, `loading.js` will be nested inside `layout.js`. It will automatically wrap the `page.js` file and any children below in a `` boundary. 77 78 85 86 ### SEO 87 88 - For bots that only scrape static HTML, and cannot execute JavaScript like a full browser, such as Twitterbot, Next.js resolves [`generateMetadata`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/generate-metadata) before streaming UI, and metadata is placed in the `` of the initial HTML. 89 - Otherwise, [streaming metadata](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/generate-metadata#streaming-metadata) may be used. Next.js automatically detects user agents to choose between blocking and streaming behavior. 90 - Since streaming is server-rendered, it does not impact SEO. You can use the [Rich Results Test](https://search.google.com/test/rich-results) tool f…(+5227)

1 --- 2 title: Dynamic Route Segments 3 nav_title: Dynamic Segments 4 description: Dynamic Route Segments can be used to programmatically generate route segments from dynamic data. 5 related: 6 title: Next Steps 7 description: For more information on what to do next, we recommend the following sections 8 links: 9 - app/api-reference/functions/generate-static-params 10 --- 11 12 When you don't know the exact route segment names ahead of time and want to create routes from dynamic data, you can use Dynamic Segments that are filled in at request time or prerendered at build time. 13 14 ## Convention 15 16 A Dynamic Segment can be created by wrapping a folder's name in square brackets: `[folderName]`. For example, a blog could include the following route `app/blog/[slug]/page.js` where `[slug]` is the Dynamic Segment for blog posts. 17 18 ```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" switcher 19 export default async function Page({ 20 params, 21 }: { 22 params: Promise<{ slug: string }> 23 }) { 24 const { slug } = await params 25 return
My Post: {slug}
26 } 27 ``` 28 29 ```jsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.js" switcher 30 export default async function Page({ params }) { 31 const { slug } = await params 32 return
My Post: {slug}
33 } 34 ``` 35 36 Dynamic Segments are passed as the `params` prop to [`layout`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/layout), [`page`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/page), [`route`](/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/route), and [`generateMetadata`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/generate-metadata#generatemetadata-function) functions. 37 38 | Route | Example URL | `params` | 39 | ------------------------- | ----------- | --------------- | 40 | `app/blog/[slug]/page.js` | `/blog/a` | `{ slug: 'a' }` | 41 | `app/blog/[slug]/page.js` | `/blog/b` | `{ slug: 'b' }` | 42 | `app/blog/[slug]/page.js` | `/blog/c` | `{ slug: 'c' }` | 43 44 ### In Client Components 45 46 In a Client Component **page**, dynamic segments from props can be accessed using the [`use`](https://react.dev/reference/react/use) API. 47 48 ```tsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.tsx" switcher 49 'use client' 50 import { use } from 'react' 51 52 export default function BlogPostPage({ 53 params, 54 }: { 55 params: Promise<{ slug: string }> 56 }) { 57 const { slug } = use(params) 58 59 return ( 60
61

{slug}

62
63 ) 64 } 65 ``` 66 67 ```jsx filename="app/blog/[slug]/page.js" switcher 68 'use client' 69 import { use } from 'react' 70 71 export default function BlogPostPage({ params }) { 72 const { slug } = use(params) 73 74 return ( 75
76

{slug}

77
78 ) 79 } 80 ``` 81 82 Alternatively Client Components can use the [`useParams`](/docs/app/api-reference/functions/use-params) hook to access the `params` anywhere in the Client Component tree. 83 84 ### Catch-all Segments 85 86 Dynamic Segments can be extended to **catch-all** subsequent segments by adding an ellipsis inside the brackets `[...folderName]`. 87 88 For example, `app/shop/[...slug]/page.js` will match `/shop/clothes`, but also `/shop/clothes/tops`, `/shop/clothes/tops/t-shirts`, and so on. 89 90 | Route | Example URL | `params` | 91 | ---------------------------- | ------------- | --------------------------- | 92 | `app/shop/[...slug]/page.js` | `/shop/a` | `{ slug: ['a'] }` | 93 | `app/shop/[...slug]/page.js` | `/shop/a/b` | `{ slug: ['a', 'b'] }` | 94 | `app/shop/[...slug]/page.js` | `/shop/a/b/c` | `{ slug: ['a', 'b', 'c'] }` | 95 96 ### Optional Catch-all Segments 97 98 Catch-all Segments can be made **optional** by including the parameter in double square brackets: `[[...folderName]]`. 99 100 For example, `app/shop/[[...slug]]/page.js` will **also** match `/shop`, in addition to `/shop/clothes`, `/shop/clothes/tops`, `/sho…(+11842)
